title: Visual arts inquiry and investigation
summary: This lesson details the foundational role of inquiry and investigation in the IB Visual Arts SL Process Portfolio. It provides strategies for developing, sustaining, and documenting a personal line of inquiry, aligning with the top-band descriptors of the assessment criteria.
learningObjectives: Analyse and interpret the work of other artists to inform personal art-making.
Document the process of experimentation with a range of media, techniques, and processes.
Synthesise research and material experiments to develop a coherent body of work.
Critically reflect on and articulate the development of personal ideas and artistic intentions.
Structure Process Portfolio screens to effectively communicate a sustained visual inquiry.

simpleExplanation.title: The Artist as Detective: Mastering Visual Inquiry
simpleExplanation.summary: Visual inquiry is the engine of your artistic practice. It's the process of asking questions about the world and seeking answers through research, experimentation, and creating art. Your Process Portfolio is the evidence file that documents this exciting investigation.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a detective and your Process Portfolio as your case file. You start with a mystery or a question (your theme). You gather clues by researching other artists and contexts. You test theories by experimenting with materials and techniques in your studio 'lab'. You follow leads, some of which are dead ends. Finally, you assemble all your evidence into a compelling narrative that solves the case, showing how your final artworks came to be.
steps: Begin with a broad theme or question and create a mind map of initial ideas, potential artists, and materials.
Conduct critical investigations into at least two artists from different cultural contexts, analysing their work's formal and conceptual qualities.
Undertake a series of purposeful material experiments, directly informed by your artist research, documenting both successes and failures.
Reflect on your journey, annotating your portfolio screens to clearly connect your research, experiments, and the refinement of your artistic intentions.

flashcards.back: The process of asking questions and seeking answers through research, reflection, and art-making. It is the driving force behind the creation of art.
The practical and theoretical research undertaken to support the inquiry. This includes analysing artists' work, exploring cultural contexts, and hands-on experimentation.
A curated selection of 9-18 screens (SL) documenting the student's art-making journey over the two-year course. It showcases experimentation, exploration, and reflection.

flashcards.front: Criterion A: Skills, techniques and processes
flashcards.back: Assesses the extent to which the student has explored and experimented with a variety of media and techniques, and demonstrated technical competence.
Assesses the depth and breadth of the student's investigation into artists, artworks, and cultural contexts, and how this has influenced their own work.

flashcards.front: Criterion C: Communication of ideas and intentions
flashcards.back: Assesses the ability to articulate ideas and intentions, and the coherence between the visual work and the written text.

flashcards.front: Criterion D: Reviewing, refining and reflecting
flashcards.back: Assesses the student's ability to review and reflect upon their work, and to refine their art-making based on this reflection.
Concise, critical written notes on Process Portfolio screens that explain processes, analyse artworks, and articulate reflections and intentions.
The elements of art (line, shape, colour, texture, form, tone, space) and principles of design (balance, rhythm, emphasis, unity, contrast) used in an artwork.
The ideas, themes, meanings, and messages embedded in an artwork. The 'why' behind the work.
A deep, ongoing, and focused exploration of a particular theme or concept over an extended period, demonstrating development and refinement.
A sketchbook or digital file used for brainstorming, initial sketches, collecting inspiration, and recording thoughts. It is the raw material from which the curated Process Portfolio is built.

faq.q: What is the difference between my visual journal/sketchbook and the Process Portfolio?
faq.a: Your visual journal is the comprehensive, often messy, day-to-day record of all your thoughts, experiments, and research. The Process Portfolio is a curated, edited, and polished presentation selected from your journal. It tells the clearest story of your artistic journey for assessment purposes. Think of the journal as the raw footage and the portfolio as the final edited film.

faq.q: How much text should I have on each screen?
faq.a: There is no strict word count, but clarity and conciseness are key. Aim for a balance where the text supports and clarifies the visuals without overwhelming them. Use annotations to explain, analyse, and reflect. A good guideline is between 100-300 words per screen, but this can vary. The quality and critical nature of the writing are more important than the quantity.

faq.q: Does my investigation have to be successful? What if I reach a dead end?
faq.a: Documenting 'failures' or changes in direction is a crucial part of a sophisticated investigation. It demonstrates critical reflection and resilience. A screen that shows an experiment that didn't work and explains *why* it didn't work and what you learned from it is evidence of a strong process. A 'dead end' is often a turning point that leads to a more fruitful path of inquiry.

faq.q: Do I have to compare artists from different cultures?
faq.a: Yes, this is a specific requirement for the higher markbands of Criterion B. The guide states that students should 'analyse and compare the work of artists from different cultural contexts'. This demonstrates a broader, more globally-minded investigation and prevents your research from becoming too narrow.

sections.content: Welcome to the core of your artistic journey in the IB Visual Arts programme. The Process Portfolio is not merely a collection of your best pieces; it is a narrative of your growth as an artist. The foundation of this narrative is your inquiry and investigation. A high-scoring portfolio is distinguished by a clear, sustained, and critically informed investigation that is evident on every screen. This lesson will equip you to move beyond simply 'making things' to developing a powerful and personal artistic inquiry.

To excel, you must understand what examiners are looking for. Your inquiry and investigation are not assessed in a single criterion; they are woven throughout the entire portfolio. Examiners want to see the 'golden thread' of your inquiry connecting everything.

items: **Criterion A (Skills, techniques and processes):** Your investigation should drive your choice of media. Don't just try different materials randomly. Your screens should show you selecting and testing media specifically to explore ideas that arose from your research. *Example: 'My investigation into Surrealist automatism led me to experiment with ink-blotting and frottage to generate unexpected forms.'*

**Criterion B (Critical investigation):** This is the heart of your inquiry. It's not enough to say you 'looked at' an artist. You must conduct a *critical* investigation, analysing how their work's formal and conceptual qualities inform your own. You must also compare artists from different contexts.

**Criterion C (Communication of ideas and intentions):** Your annotations must clearly articulate the link between your investigation and your own developing intentions. Use subject-specific language to explain how your research has shaped your ideas.

**Criterion D (Reviewing, refining and reflecting):** Top-band work shows a constant cycle of action and reflection. Your investigation should not be a one-time event at the start. It should be ongoing, with later screens showing how you revisited research or sought new artists to solve problems that arose in your studio work.

sections.content: Every great body of work starts with a spark of curiosity. Your first task is to transform a broad interest into a focused line of inquiry. Avoid generic themes like 'nature' or 'identity'. Instead, narrow them down into questions. Instead of 'identity', ask: 'How do inherited objects shape our sense of self?' Instead of 'nature', ask: 'How can I represent the tension between natural growth and urban infrastructure?'

sections.question: Model the initial investigation stage for a Process Portfolio, starting with the theme 'Memory and Place'. Create annotations for a screen showing a mind map and an initial artist analysis.
sections.solution:
*(This would be presented on a portfolio screen with visuals of a mind map and a work by the chosen artist)*

**Annotation 1 (next to mind map):**
'My inquiry begins with the theme of 'Memory and Place', specifically focusing on how childhood spaces are remembered and distorted over time. This mind map explores initial conceptual pathways (nostalgia vs. trauma, architectural traces, fragmented narratives) and potential material processes (photo transfer, collage, layering). I have identified artists like Rachel Whiteread, who casts negative space, and Christian Boltanski, who uses archival imagery, as initial points of investigation.'

**Annotation 2 (next to an image of Rachel Whiteread's 'House'):**
'**Critical Investigation:** Rachel Whiteread's 'House' (1993) is a profound influence. Formally, its monolithic, brutalist presence is a powerful fossil of a domestic interior. Conceptually, she makes memory tangible by solidifying absence. This challenges me to think beyond two-dimensional representations of memory. Her process of casting directly informs my intention to experiment with plaster and resin to capture the 'imprint' of objects and spaces, rather than just depicting them. This moves my inquiry from simple illustration towards a more sculptural and conceptual approach.'

sections.content: Stage 2: Sustaining the Investigation Through Experimentation

This is where your research meets the studio. A sustained investigation is a dynamic cycle: you research an artist, which gives you an idea for a material experiment. The result of that experiment (whether a success or 'happy accident' or failure) raises new questions, which might send you back to your research or onto a new experiment. Your portfolio screens must document this cycle clearly. Show your thought process.

items: **Purposeful Play:** Your experiments should not be random. They must be driven by your investigation. Always ask: 'Why am I doing this? What question am I trying to answer with this experiment?'

**Document Everything:** Photograph your process at different stages. A screen showing a failed photo transfer next to a successful one, with annotations explaining what you learned, is far more valuable than a screen with only perfect outcomes.

**Analyse, Don't Just Describe:** Instead of 'I tried using watercolour', write 'In an attempt to capture the ephemeral quality of memory identified in my research, I experimented with wet-on-wet watercolour techniques. However, the lack of control proved ineffective for rendering the architectural details central to my concept.'

**Connect to Context:** When you investigate an artist, consider their context. How did their time and place influence their work? How does your own context differ, and how does that affect how you interpret their ideas in your own work?

sections.content: Examiners reward a clear narrative structure. Think of your portfolio screens as pages in a book. Each screen should logically follow the last and set up the next. Use your annotations as the narrator's voice, guiding the examiner through your journey of discovery, struggle, and breakthrough. A portfolio that shows a clear, connected path of inquiry will always score higher than one that feels like a random collection of disconnected experiments.

sections.question: Create a model annotation for a Process Portfolio screen that connects an investigation into the artist El Anatsui with a personal material experiment.
sections.solution:
*(This would be on a screen showing an image of an El Anatsui tapestry and photos of the student's own experiment with found materials)*

'**Investigation & Material Development:** My research into Ghanaian artist El Anatsui's bottle-cap tapestries revealed his interest in transforming discarded materials to comment on trade, history, and the environment. I was drawn to the formal qualities of his work – the shimmering, cloth-like surfaces created from rigid metal.

**Experiment:** Inspired by his process of 'upcycling', I decided to explore materials from my own environment. I collected used tea bags, a symbol of domestic ritual. My initial attempts to stitch them together (see photo, left) were fragile and lacked visual impact. Reflecting on Anatsui's use of copper wire to create a strong, flexible structure, I began weaving the tea bags together with fine-gauge wire (see photo, right). This process not only provided structural integrity but also introduced a metallic element that contrasts with the organic paper, creating a dialogue between the industrial and the domestic that aligns with my developing conceptual intentions.'

sections.content: As your investigation progresses, you will generate a large volume of research, experiments, and developmental studies. The final stage of the inquiry process involves stepping back, reviewing this body of work, and making critical decisions. This is where Criterion D is most evident. You must show that you can look at your own work with a critical eye, identify strengths and weaknesses, and use these insights to refine your ideas and create more resolved artworks. Your inquiry doesn't stop; it becomes more focused, leading towards the resolved works for your Exhibition.

items: **Curate Your Screens:** You cannot include everything. Select the screens that best tell the story of your investigation. Show the most important turning points, breakthroughs, and refinements.

**Show 'Before and After':** A powerful way to demonstrate refinement is to show an early, less successful piece next to a later, more resolved version, with annotations explaining the changes you made and why.

**Articulate Your Intentions:** As you near the end of the process, your intentions should become clearer. Your later screens should articulate what you are trying to communicate conceptually in your work.

**The Link to Exhibition:** The Process Portfolio should demonstrate the journey that leads to the resolved works in your Exhibition. The final screens can act as a bridge, showing the final developments and reflections that culminated in your exhibition pieces.

sections.label: Test Your Understanding of Visual Inquiry
